speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 14th, 1996. Dorothy Grinims is being interviewed by Abby Ellin in Jersey City, New Jersey, United States, in English. Today is January 14, 1996. Dorothy Grinim is being interviewed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Grinims.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by Abby Ellin. Before we begin, I'd like you to tell me your name and spell it, and your maiden name, and also your parents' name, and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dorothy Grinims. I was born in Ostrog, Poland, February 16th, 1925. My father's name was Lazar Zeigerson, Z-E-I-G-E-R-S-O-N. My mother's name was Syma, S-Y-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great. Tell me about your childhood. What did-- what did your father do for a living? What was your town like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- basically my father was a Talmudic scholar, and very Orthodox. And he was greatly occupied with the synagogues. He was not an ordained rabbi, but he studied to be one. And he was a-- had a small factory, manufacturing chairs and tables. He employed maybe two or three people. In those days you didn't have big businesses. And [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- and so you were religious? Your family was very Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he teach you about Judaism? Did you learn Hebrew with him? Did you study the Talmud?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. I was a-- I went to Hebrew school and I studied religion. And I was very observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about brothers and sisters? How many did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three brothers and one sister. Two brothers immigrated to the United States in the '20s. And I was raised with my-- my brother Sidney and my sister Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And tell me about your house. What-- what did it look like? What do you remember from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a large house, but it was a very comfortable house. And we had a very nice home life. There was a lot of culture, a lot of religion, and basically very decent, honest, moral people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- do you remember like a favorite toy that you had as a child? Something that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother-- we didn't have toys like my granddaughter has today. But we had like a yo-yo, a ball. My-- my father always used to-- I loved to play ball outside. So I was very athletic. And so I played ball. And then bicycle riding and sleigh riding and ice skating. And that's all I can remember about my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is Ostrog like? What was it like, the town? Was it big, small?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- was a town, not big. It was a-- the population was 30,000, 10,000 Jews, and the rest Ukrainians and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's near?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the Russian border, a few miles on the Russian border, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Jewish community very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community was very close. We had a large Jewish community. And we had many synagogues and we had yeshivas. We had a-- Zionist organizations, many. And I was [INAUDIBLE] within a Zionist organization as a-- as a young girl, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- and did you go to Hebrew school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hebrew school. And first I went to a-- to a Polish school. All the subjects were in Polish. And later, high school was Hebrew. All the subjects were in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you had a sense of yourself as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so, yes, identity as a Jew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish holidays? Can you describe-- what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We observed all the Jewish holidays. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was like, in what respect? I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there lots of family gathered, relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just-- just the five of us, the five, two brothers who were here when I was growing up. And we used to gather at a table and celebrate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Passover, and Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and all the other-- Sukkos, my father used to build a sukkot outside the house and we ate in the sukkot. So it was basically a very Jewish traditional family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had many non-Jewish friends from this Polish school. And I had a lot of Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always liked to be surrounded with many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when did things first start to change in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life started to become very tragic from the age of 14. That's when-- when the war broke out. In 1939, September 1st, 1939, when the Russians occupied our town and part of the eastern part of Poland, and that's when my life changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened, the specific moment? What-- what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? We were sound asleep at 4 o'clock in the morning. We heard tanks in-- in the main road. And everybody was extremely frightened. It was unexpectedly. And we woke up and got dressed and we went out to see what was going on. And we saw-- all of a sudden, we saw Russian soldiers on tanks in the-- in the street. They didn't bother anybody. They were very friendly. They gave out cigarettes and they gave out matches. And we didn't know what happened. But then we found out that's when the war started, well, that's when Hitler made a pact with Stalin that Russia would take over eastern part and Germany the western part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in with tanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in the tanks and they occupied our city. And at that time, it became-- we were under the Russian domination. Domination?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: They changed that they immediately were arresting and sending to Siberia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-to-do people, businesspeople. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were persecuting the Zionists. Many of them fled. They were persecuting the well-to-do people. Only the working people had very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stopped going to school? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. School started again, school immediately. Everything from Polish school or Hebrew school, everything was a-- a transition to Russian, Russian schools. Everything was in Russian. We had to learn Russian right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was like? It was awful, because we didn't have any money. We didn't have an income. There were lines all night. We had to stay in lines for food. I remember I was 14 years old and-- and I was on line all night in cold weather to get sugar and-- and essentials, just sugar and-- and milk and bread. It was very, very bad. It was-- we were-- that's the time when we-- our starvation practically started. We had very little food. And of course everyone was very fearful, didn't know what they're going to do to us. But we as children had-- we had very-- a very nice life, I would say. There was a lot of dancing and singing and then going to school. And they really tried to indoctrinate Communism into us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is when the Russians were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From 1939 'til '41, two years, we are under Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still practice Judaism? Did your father still-- to temples, everything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I go to temple yes. You did? Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Back then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in-- no. Everything stopped. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But secretly, did you-- at home, would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Secretly, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: For that reason, the Christians didn't practice either, because, you know, atheism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened? So they were there for two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there for two years. And then the war broke out between Russia and Germany. And then one day we woke up and then we saw parachutes and Germans landing from the parachutes. And everybody started to escape. The Russians tried to save the people, mainly Jewish people. And they chased them out of their homes to go to Russia. But we remained in our house. My brother and sister escaped to Russia on a-- on a truck. And my-- and I decided I'll stay with my parents. I felt somebody should be with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't they-- they just didn't want to go to Russia? They just didn't want to uproot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believed at the time what the Nazis are going to do to the Jews. We just thought it's just a temporary war. And so we-- we remained, my father and I, and my mother. And I have to stop, because I'm getting very nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Excuse me. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was I?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're telling about living with your parents. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother and sister escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when the-- yes. My brother and sister escaped, and I lived with my parents. And then-- then there was a six-day-- six-day fighting in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get back-- so your sister and brother escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: They escaped to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed with your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things change then in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then it-- then the war was on. There were fights. It was a-- it was a war between Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember specifics, what you saw in the town, things--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our town was completely engulfed in flames. We were running from corner to corner. Every place they turned around, all fires, because they bombarded fiercely our town. The reason for it was because our town was a strategic place for them, because it was at the border with Russia. That's why the Germans fought. And the Russians, they didn't-- Russians didn't want to give up the town, Ostrog. And I witnessed from my window street fights. I saw Russian soldiers-- soldiers and German soldiers. And they were fighting with bayonets, And then when the-- when the Russians-- when the Germans left, the Russian soldiers were dying in front of our-- my house. And they were wounded and they were crying in Russian, [RUSSIAN]. That means water. And-- and I tried to get out and give them water. At this point, I heard shots again, and I went in. And then he died practically-- from my window I saw him die in front of my eyes. This-- this moment I'll never forget. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is when you were about what, 16 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try to remember now.

speaker: interviewer
text: More or less.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 16 years, two years, right, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- and then the Russians defeated and then the Germans took over our town. And we were already under the Russian-- under the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, and then it started, the-- you want me to tell you what the Germans did at that point? There was-- they formed the Jewish committee. And through this Jewish committee, they gave out orders, the-- to the Jewish people to deliver all the gold and silver, leather to the German city government, which we did. And after that, all the Jews were forced to wear yellow patches. So we all had to wear a yellow patch in front and on the back for identity, the Jewish identity. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And after a month later started the first execution, first [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- before you tell me about that, what were you thinking? I mean, you had to all of a sudden wear this yellow patch--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. You-- you-- slowly they prepared us for everything. You were just in a shock, [INAUDIBLE]. You just don't think. We didn't think. I don't remember thinking. You would just fear and you had to obey orders, or else you were told if you're not going to wear it, you'll be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they prepare it for you slowly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- I mean, first-- it was sort of step by step. First, there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: First-- first with the yellow patches, then rations. We didn't have-- we didn't have food. We were only allowed like a loaf of bread to a family. And they were just-- terrorized us. And of course, the Ukrainians collaborated at the time with the Germans. And they formed the Ukrainian police, not all Ukrainians, of course, but many did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Ukrainian police, they-- they were coming to Jewish homes and just terrorized, you know. They didn't-- there was no mass-- mass killings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They came to our house and they searched in the closets and they opened up doors and they-- just walking around. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just looking for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just looking for-- for leather, for money, for gold, for silver, so forth. And then a month later started the first [NON-ENGLISH], the first execution. And we woke up one morning and we heard footsteps. Those were notorious footsteps of the Nazis. And they were screaming in the streets, [GERMAN]. That means get out. And they chased everybody. This was like a death march. They chased us all to this-- to the forests where the first execution was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- you were-- we were talking about that earlier, the death march. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I take one break for a second? No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have some water. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm no good. I didn't get enough sleep. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. You OK? OK. So you were telling about the march. What-- what do you remember from that march when you had to go into the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: They chased everybody, chased my mother and father. And I ran down. I ran away. And one Nazi caught me. My father and mother had gone. And because I tried to run away, he placed a machine gun in the back of me. And for about a mile, a mile and a half, he was in the back of me, escorting me to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were marching with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone. And I saw from distance there were-- and as I was walking, marching, I was stepping-- I stepped on all the furs, jewelry, because people took their possessions. They thought maybe they're being relocated. But they made them drop everything, so when they got to this place, they were-- they didn't have any possessions with them. And I marched with this Nazi. And then I came to the place. I found my mother. My father was already separated. They immediately took the older men, the religious people, the sick people from hospitals, and they separated them from the women and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was all day with my mother on a very hot day, in the hot sun, no water, no food, just standing and screaming and crying. And everybody else did. And I remember distinctly black birds flying all over us. And I asked my mother, what are all the black birds doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, that's a bad sign. That means they're going to kill us here. Black birds usually come when there is a tragedy, when there is disaster. And we were there from early in the morning until late in the evening. And then 'til-- we were surrounded by all the Nazis, all-- and Ukrainian police. And then an order came that the women and children can go home. And my mother and I went back to the house. And my father was no longer with us. And then the next morning, I got up-- got up very early. And I-- I heard that they took some people to a-- to a different area town, like the prison. As I-- as I was walking and crying, I was approached by a Ukrainian policeman. His name was Lusik Stepaniuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had a rifle in the back-- on the back. He was on a bicycle. And he stopped me. He asked me, why are you crying? Where are you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, my father, I'm looking for my father. We were told that-- last night that some people were taken to-- to prison. He says, don't go there. They were all shot. I was there. They were all killed. And then he asked me where I lived. And then he said he'll come over in the evening. He came in the evening. And he befriended me and my mother and my neighbors. And he said, I'll be coming every night to tell you, to give you a warning if there was going to be another execution, which they expect to-- to happen. And then he brought over his mother. And he said to his mother that-- that if anything should happen to him, that she should save me and my mother. And she brought us food. And-- [INAUDIBLE]. And then we lived in this house, my mother and I, with my neighbors, friends, until the second execution. That was a few-- a few months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- before-- I want to know, you walked for a mile and a half with a rifle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sticking into your back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: You are numb. You're shocked. It's-- you're just-- you're just-- it's fear. You're just-- your heart palpates and you just don't know anything. You're like in a-- like unconscious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you think that was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it's very hard to explain. It's such horrendous fear. It's such a horrible feeling that I-- I never-- I never had this feeling again in my life. It's the worst feeling you can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you were back in your house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back in the house. And then there was a second execution. And then they chased everybody out again of the houses. And then my mother was taken first. And I was able-- I was separated from my mother and I ran. And I opened the door from the basement. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: They chased you out into the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: They chased-- they chased them-- I didn't know where they chased-- where-- where to, because I remained in the house. They got my mother first. And I was able to run into the cellar. And I closed the door on the inside of the cellar, so they were able-- they knew that somebody's there, because I should've left the door open, which I didn't. I left it-- I locked from the inside in the cellar. And I was sitting in the cellar. And-- and then I heard footsteps in the house. Again, [GERMAN]. And then somebody tried to open the door from the cellar. And he was banging with the rifle. At this point, I felt it's no use. My mother was gone and everybody else is gone. I might as well go to. I I felt there's no point to-- to remain alive. As I came out from the cellar, in front of me was this Ukrainian policeman with a big rifle. And he said, out. And I went on my knees and I cried. And I said please, let me live. I want to live. I'm only 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, go back into the cellar but leave the door open. He remained in the house. Then several Nazis-- I heard the footsteps-- several Nazis came in into the house. And they asked him in German, is everybody [GERMAN]? And he said yes, everybody's [GERMAN]. And they left. And he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all day in the cellar. And then towards the evening, I said, I'm going to get myself out and I don't want to live. As I ran out through the back of the cellar, I saw my mother running up the hill. And she was black. Her face was black. I never saw her look like this. And she said, they took all your friends, all the young people they took on trucks. And she was screaming and crying. And we embraced. And we came back to the house. This was the second execution, where they took all the young people, all my friends, all my young relatives they took on trucks and we didn't know where they took them. Some say that they were gassed in the trucks and some say they were shot. This, I don't have no recollection of that. That was the second [NON-ENGLISH]. And after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave the house ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go out of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went out of the house, yes, because after that, an order came out they're going to place us in a ghetto. We had to pack our belongings and move to a ghetto, which was a-- a neighborhood where very poor Jews lived years ago. And there was this big, very famous synagogue, [NON-ENGLISH], it was called, [NON-ENGLISH]. Maharsha. And we lived in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just called Ostrog ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just trying to see it. Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were-- we-- they gave us one room and a kitchen to share with-- with other people. It was a small room and my mother and I slept in one room. And we were practically starving. There was no food. Whoever tried to escape from the ghetto to get food, they were shot in front of that synagogue. All the executions were in front of the synagogue, because we were told that this one was shot and all our other friends were shot in front of the synagogue, this big [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was no food. And it was despair. And it was hunger, was-- was horrendous. It was-- it's impossible to describe. And we lived-- it was a day to day, an hour from hour, a day to day existence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- until started the third execution, the Final Solution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody worked. Nobody worked. It was a different ghetto than other ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had rations. We were on line for just little, little food. But we were able to get-- my mother had friends, Ukrainian friends. And somehow they were able to smuggle some food to my mother, but very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know how they did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did it near a gate, because we were-- the ghetto had barbed wires. There was a gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came-- they came to the gate and they were able to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time elapsed between the second execution and the third?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my husband will give you the information. I just don't recall right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so you were in the ghetto, let's say, for however long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, however long. And then my husband-- at that time, he was my friend from school. We were friends. And he was able to, at the last execution-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the last execution, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we heard shots. And then we knew that this is the Final Solution.
